Melanoma — Basic Information

Disease Name: Melanoma
Definition: Melanoma is a malignant tumor arising from melanocytes — the pigment-producing cells of the skin (and occasionally mucous membranes, eyes, and other sites). It is the most dangerous form of skin cancer due to its high metastatic potential.
Overview: Melanoma accounts for only ~1% of skin cancers but causes the majority of skin cancer deaths. It can occur anywhere on the body, including areas not exposed to sunlight. While UV radiation is the primary environmental cause, genetic predispositions play a significant role. Early detection is critical, as localized melanoma has an excellent prognosis whereas metastatic melanoma has historically been fatal, though immunotherapy and targeted therapy have dramatically improved outcomes.

Melanoma — Causes

Causes: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation exposure (sunlight, tanning beds)
DNA damage in melanocytes leading to BRAF, NRAS, NF1, or KIT mutations
Somatic mutations accumulating in melanocytic nevi
Rare germline mutations (CDKN2A, CDK4, BAP1)
Risk Factors: Fair skin, light hair, blue/green eyes
History of sunburns (especially blistering sunburns in childhood)
High UV exposure (geographic latitude, outdoor occupation)
Tanning bed use
Large number of moles (>50 common nevi)
Atypical (dysplastic) nevi
Personal or family history of melanoma
Immunosuppression (organ transplant recipients, HIV)
CDKN2A germline mutation
Xeroderma pigmentosum

Melanoma — Pathophysiology

Disease Mechanism: Melanoma develops from transformed melanocytes that acquire sequential genetic mutations disrupting cell cycle regulation, apoptosis, and invasion. The MAPK signaling pathway (RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK) is activated in most melanomas, predominantly through BRAF V600E mutation. Tumor progression follows a radial growth phase (horizontal spread within epidermis) then vertical growth phase (dermal invasion), enabling metastasis via lymphatics and blood.
Cellular Changes: BRAF V600E mutation (~50% of cutaneous melanomas) — constitutive MAPK activation
NRAS mutations (~20%) — activates MAPK and PI3K pathways
NF1 loss-of-function mutations (~15%) — RAS hyperactivation
CDKN2A deletion — loss of p16 tumor suppressor, cell cycle dysregulation
TERT promoter mutation — telomerase reactivation enabling immortalization
Epithelial-mesenchymal transition enabling invasion
MHC downregulation for immune evasion
PD-L1 upregulation — suppression of cytotoxic T-cell response

Melanoma — Classification

Types: Superficial Spreading Melanoma: Most common type (~70%); horizontal radial growth phase; irregular borders and color variation; occurs on trunk (men) and legs (women).
Nodular Melanoma: ~15-20%; aggressive vertical growth from outset; often amelanotic; rapidly enlarging raised nodule.
Lentigo Maligna Melanoma: ~5-10%; occurs on chronically sun-damaged skin (face, neck) in older adults; slow-growing precursor lentigo maligna.
Acral Lentiginous Melanoma: Occurs on palms, soles, subungual areas; most common melanoma in dark-skinned populations; associated with KIT mutations.
Mucosal Melanoma: Rare; occurs in oral, nasal, anogenital, or gastrointestinal mucosa; poor prognosis.
Uveal Melanoma: Arises in choroid, ciliary body, or iris; high risk of liver metastasis; associated with GNA11/GNAQ mutations.
Desmoplastic Melanoma: Rare; neurotropic, spindle cell variant; occurs in head/neck; high local recurrence.

Melanoma — Clinical Presentation

Early Symptoms: New or changing mole
Asymmetric pigmented lesion (ABCDE criteria: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6mm, Evolution/change)
Itching or tingling in existing mole
Darkening or color change in a skin lesion
Common Symptoms: Irregular, multicolored pigmented lesion (shades of brown, black, red, white, blue)
Irregular, notched borders
Bleeding, ulceration, or crusting of a lesion
Satellite lesions around primary tumor
Enlarged regional lymph nodes
Advanced Symptoms: Palpable lymph node metastases (regional)
Bone pain (skeletal metastases)
Headache, seizures, focal neurological deficits (brain metastases)
Shortness of breath (pulmonary metastases)
Weight loss, fatigue, anorexia (systemic disease)
Abdominal pain (liver or GI metastases)
Elevated LDH (associated with widespread metastatic disease)

Melanoma — Diagnosis

Physical Examination: Full skin examination (total body skin exam)
Dermoscopy (dermatoscopy) — enhances visualization of subsurface structures
Lymph node palpation
Abdominal examination for hepatomegaly
Laboratory Tests: Blood Tests: LDH — prognostic marker in metastatic melanoma
S100B protein — melanoma-specific biomarker
Melanoma inhibitory activity (MIA) protein
CBC and metabolic panel — baseline before systemic therapy
Imaging: Ultrasound of regional lymph nodes
CT scan (chest/abdomen/pelvis) — metastasis staging
PET-CT scan — whole-body staging in stage III-IV
MRI brain — detect cerebral metastases
Bone scan — if bone metastasis suspected
Biopsy: Excisional biopsy with 1-3mm margins — gold standard for primary diagnosis. Incisional biopsy acceptable for large lesions. Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) for tumors >1mm Breslow thickness.
Histopathological Examination: H&E staining of excised specimen; Breslow thickness measurement (mm); Clark level; ulceration assessment; mitotic rate; lymphovascular invasion; microsatellites; margins assessment

Melanoma — Histopathology

Microscopic Findings: Atypical melanocytes at dermal-epidermal junction arranged in irregular nests
Upward (pagetoid) spread of melanocytes into epidermis (superficial spreading type)
Vertical growth phase: atypical melanocytes invading dermis and forming expansile nodule
Nuclear pleomorphism, prominent eosinophilic nucleoli ('owl eyes')
High mitotic rate including atypical mitoses
Ulceration of overlying epidermis
Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) — variable and prognostically relevant
Desmoplastic melanoma: spindle cells in fibrous stroma, neurotropism
Tissue Changes: Epidermal consumption and ulceration
Dermal invasion with displacement of collagen
Lymphovascular invasion
Perineural invasion (desmoplastic type)
Regression areas (fibrosis, melanophages, telangiectasia)
Cellular Characteristics: Epithelioid, spindled, or mixed cell morphology
Abundant finely granular cytoplasm (may be pigmented)
Large, irregular, hyperchromatic nuclei
Prominent nucleoli
Positive for S100, SOX10, HMB-45, Melan-A/MART-1 by IHC

Melanoma — Biomarkers

Diagnostic Biomarkers: S100B — most sensitive melanoma marker by IHC
HMB-45 (gp100) — melanoma-specific, less sensitive
Melan-A/MART-1 — specific for melanocytic differentiation
SOX10 — highly sensitive transcription factor marker
MiTF (Microphthalmia transcription factor)
PNL2 — additional melanocytic marker
Prognostic Biomarkers: Breslow thickness (primary prognostic factor)
Ulceration (worsens prognosis, upstages T category)
Mitotic rate (>/=1/mm² adversely affects prognosis)
LDH level (elevated = stage M1c in metastatic disease)
Sentinel lymph node status
PD-L1 expression (predictive of immunotherapy response)
Genetic Markers: BRAF V600E/V600K mutation — present in ~50%; predictive of BRAF inhibitor response
NRAS Q61 mutation — ~20%; may predict MEK inhibitor response
NF1 mutation — ~15%; defines 'triple-wild-type' subgroup
KIT mutation/amplification — acral/mucosal subtypes; imatinib-sensitive
CDKN2A deletion — germline mutation in familial melanoma
GNAQ/GNA11 — uveal melanoma; MEK inhibitor target
TMB (Tumor Mutational Burden) — predictive of immunotherapy response

Melanoma — Treatment

First Line Treatment: Wide local excision (WLE) with appropriate surgical margins based on Breslow thickness for localized disease. Systemic therapy (immunotherapy or targeted therapy) for stage III-IV disease.
Medications: Pembrolizumab — anti-PD-1 immunotherapy (adjuvant and advanced disease)
Nivolumab — anti-PD-1 immunotherapy
Ipilimumab — anti-CTLA-4 immunotherapy
Dabrafenib + Trametinib — BRAF+MEK inhibitor combination (BRAF-mutant)
Vemurafenib + Cobimetinib — BRAF+MEK inhibitor combination
Encorafenib + Binimetinib — BRAF+MEK inhibitor combination
Surgery: Wide local excision (WLE): margins 0.5cm for in situ; 1cm for T1; 1-2cm for T2; 2cm for T3-T4
Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) — for tumors >1mm Breslow thickness
Complete lymph node dissection (CLND) — when SLNB positive (selective use)
Resection of isolated metastases (oligometastatic disease)
Surgery for brain metastases
Chemotherapy: Dacarbazine (DTIC) — historical standard; now rarely used
Temozolomide — oral alkylating agent (cross BBB for CNS disease)
Carboplatin + paclitaxel — occasional salvage use
Chemotherapy largely replaced by immunotherapy and targeted therapy
Radiotherapy: Adjuvant radiotherapy to nodal basin after high-risk lymph node dissection
Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) — brain metastases
Whole-brain radiotherapy (WBRT) — multiple brain metastases
Palliative radiation for bone, soft tissue metastases
Immunotherapy: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) — FDA-approved for adjuvant stage IIB-IV and advanced melanoma
Nivolumab (anti-PD-1) — FDA-approved for adjuvant and advanced melanoma
Ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) — used in combination with nivolumab
Nivolumab + Ipilimumab — superior PFS/OS in metastatic melanoma vs. monotherapy
Talimogene laherparepvec (T-VEC) — oncolytic virus (intralesional injection for unresectable stage IIIB-IVM1a)
Relatlimab + Nivolumab (anti-LAG-3 + anti-PD-1) — approved for unresectable/metastatic melanoma
Targeted Therapy: BRAF inhibitors: Vemurafenib, Dabrafenib, Encorafenib
MEK inhibitors: Trametinib, Cobimetinib, Binimetinib
BRAF+MEK combination preferred over monotherapy (prevents resistance)
KIT inhibitors (Imatinib) — KIT-mutant acral/mucosal melanoma
MEK inhibitors (Trametinib, Binimetinib, Selumetinib) — uveal melanoma (investigational)

Melanoma — Complications

Short Term: Surgical wound complications (infection, hematoma, seroma, dehiscence)
Lymphedema after lymph node dissection
Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) from immunotherapy: colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathy, dermatitis
Skin toxicity from BRAF/MEK inhibitors (rash, photosensitivity, squamous cell carcinomas)
Fever syndrome from BRAF inhibitors
Long Term: Lymphedema (persistent after lymphadenectomy)
Secondary cutaneous malignancies (squamous cell carcinoma from vemurafenib)
Permanent endocrine dysfunction (hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency from immunotherapy)
Chronic immune-related adverse events
Psychological impact and surveillance anxiety
Brain metastases with neurological sequelae
Acquired resistance to targeted therapy (median ~10-12 months)

Melanoma — Prevention

Prevention Methods: UV protection: sunscreen (SPF 30+), protective clothing, hats
Avoid midday sun exposure (10 AM – 4 PM)
Avoidance of tanning beds (linked to ~75% increased risk)
Regular skin self-examination using ABCDE criteria
Genetic counseling for CDKN2A mutation carriers
Lifestyle Modifications: Daily broad-spectrum sunscreen application
Wearing UV-protective clothing and wide-brimmed hats
Seeking shade during peak UV hours
Avoiding intentional tanning
Vitamin D supplementation to compensate for sun avoidance
Screening Recommendations: Annual total body skin examination by dermatologist for high-risk individuals
Genetic testing and surveillance for CDKN2A mutation families
Dermoscopy-enhanced monitoring for atypical nevi
Monthly self-skin examination recommended by AAD
Ophthalmologic monitoring for uveal melanoma risk factors

Melanoma — Prognosis

Survival Rate: Stage I: ~95-99% 5-year OS; Stage II: ~65-85%; Stage III: ~40-70% (varies by substage); Stage IV (metastatic): historically <10% at 5 years, now ~50% with modern immunotherapy combinations.
Disease Progression: Melanoma can progress rapidly from localized to metastatic disease. Common metastatic sites include regional lymph nodes, skin (in-transit/satellite), lung, brain, liver, and bone. Brain metastases occur in up to 50% of patients with advanced disease.
Recurrence: Recurrence risk correlates with Breslow thickness and lymph node status. Most recurrences occur within 5 years. Adjuvant immunotherapy reduces recurrence risk by ~35-40%. Lifelong surveillance is recommended.

Lung Cancer — Basic Information

Disease Name: Lung Cancer
Definition: Lung cancer is a malignant neoplasm originating from the epithelial cells of the lung. It is the leading cause of cancer-related death worldwide, broadly classified into non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and small cell lung cancer (SCLC).
Overview: Lung cancer is responsible for more deaths annually than breast, colorectal, and prostate cancers combined. The overwhelming majority (~85%) of cases are attributed to tobacco smoking. Advances in molecular profiling have transformed treatment, with targeted therapies for specific driver mutations (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, MET, RET, KRAS) and immunotherapy significantly improving survival in selected patients.

Lung Cancer — Causes

Causes: Tobacco smoking (primary cause; responsible for ~80-85% of lung cancers)
Secondhand smoke exposure
Radon gas exposure (leading cause in non-smokers)
Occupational carcinogens (asbestos, arsenic, chromium, nickel, diesel fumes)
Air pollution (particulate matter PM2.5)
Genetic susceptibility and driver mutations
Prior lung diseases (COPD, pulmonary fibrosis, tuberculosis)
Prior radiation therapy to chest
Risk Factors: Tobacco smoking (current or former; pack-year history)
Age >45 years
Family history of lung cancer
Occupational exposure to asbestos, radon, heavy metals
Living in high air pollution areas
Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)
Pulmonary fibrosis
HIV infection
Genetic mutations (EGFR, KRAS germline variants in some families)

Lung Cancer — Pathophysiology

Disease Mechanism: Lung carcinogenesis is a multi-step process initiated by carcinogen-induced DNA damage (primarily from tobacco smoke containing 60+ known carcinogens). Key oncogenic pathways include EGFR-RAS-MAPK, PI3K-AKT-mTOR, and ALK signaling. Tumor suppressor inactivation (TP53, RB1, CDKN2A) combined with oncogene activation leads to uncontrolled cell proliferation, invasion, and metastasis.
Cellular Changes: KRAS mutations — most common in smokers with adenocarcinoma (~30%)
EGFR activating mutations — exon 19 deletion, exon 21 L858R (~15% overall; higher in Asian non-smokers, women)
ALK rearrangements — EML4-ALK fusion (~5%)
ROS1 rearrangements (~2%)
BRAF V600E mutations (~2-3%)
MET exon 14 skipping mutations (~3-4%)
RET fusions (~2%)
NTRK fusions (rare)
TP53 mutation (most common tumor suppressor loss)
RB1 loss — near universal in SCLC
PD-L1 overexpression — immune checkpoint evasion

Lung Cancer — Classification

Types: Nsclc: Adenocarcinoma: Most common NSCLC (~40% of all lung cancers); peripheral location; associated with EGFR, ALK, ROS1, KRAS mutations; predominant type in non-smokers and women.
Squamous Cell Carcinoma: ~25-30% of NSCLC; central location near bronchi; strongly linked to smoking; FGFR1 amplification, DDR2 mutations.
Large Cell Carcinoma: ~10-15%; poorly differentiated; diagnosis of exclusion; rapid growth.
Other Nsclc: Adenosquamous, sarcomatoid carcinoma, carcinoid tumors
Sclc: Small Cell Lung Cancer (~15%); neuroendocrine origin; very aggressive; rapid growth; early metastasis; paraneoplastic syndromes; almost exclusively in smokers; classified as limited-stage or extensive-stage.

Lung Cancer — Clinical Presentation

Early Symptoms: Persistent new cough or change in chronic cough
Mild dyspnea on exertion
Incidental finding on chest imaging
Often asymptomatic in early stages
Common Symptoms: Hemoptysis (coughing blood)
Chest pain (pleuritic or dull, persistent)
Progressive dyspnea
Unexplained weight loss
Fatigue and loss of appetite
Recurrent respiratory infections (pneumonia, bronchitis)
Wheezing or stridor
Advanced Symptoms: Hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement)
Dysphagia (esophageal compression)
Superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome (facial/arm swelling, plethora)
Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) — Pancoast tumor
Bone pain (skeletal metastases)
Headache, seizures (brain metastases — common in adenocarcinoma, SCLC)
Jaundice (liver metastases)
Paraneoplastic syndromes: SIADH (SCLC), Cushing's syndrome, Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome, hypercalcemia (squamous cell)

Lung Cancer — Diagnosis

Physical Examination: Decreased breath sounds, dullness to percussion (pleural effusion)
Signs of SVC syndrome
Lymphadenopathy (supraclavicular, cervical)
Finger clubbing
Signs of Horner's syndrome
Laboratory Tests: Blood Tests: CBC — anemia of chronic disease
Comprehensive metabolic panel — liver function, calcium level
LDH — tumor burden marker
CEA (carcinoembryonic antigen) — elevated in adenocarcinoma
NSE (neuron-specific enolase) — SCLC marker
ProGRP (pro-gastrin-releasing peptide) — SCLC marker
Imaging: Chest X-ray — initial evaluation (mass, effusion, atelectasis)
CT chest with contrast — primary tumor characterization, lymph nodes
PET-CT — whole-body staging (M staging)
MRI brain — brain metastasis detection (mandatory before curative intent treatment)
Bone scan — skeletal metastases (if PET not available)
CT-guided biopsy — peripheral lesions
Endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS) — mediastinal staging
Biopsy: Bronchoscopy with BAL, brushings, biopsy — central tumors. CT-guided percutaneous needle biopsy — peripheral lesions. Endobronchial ultrasound-guided biopsy (EBUS-TBNA) — mediastinal/hilar lymph nodes. Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) — pleural disease or diagnostic lobectomy.
Histopathological Examination: H&E staining, IHC panel (TTF-1, p40/p63, CK5/6, synaptophysin, CD56, chromogranin); molecular profiling for driver mutations; PD-L1 expression by TPS scoring; TMB assessment

Lung Cancer — Histopathology

Microscopic Findings: Adenocarcinoma: acinar, papillary, micropapillary, lepidic, or solid growth patterns; mucin production; TTF-1 positive; lepidic pattern (former BAC) grows along alveolar walls
Squamous cell carcinoma: keratinization, intercellular bridges, pearls; p40/p63/CK5/6 positive
SCLC: sheets of small cells with scant cytoplasm, 'salt and pepper' chromatin, nuclear molding, extensive necrosis; Ki-67 >70%; synaptophysin/CD56/chromogranin positive
Large cell carcinoma: sheets of large pleomorphic cells without squamous/glandular/neuroendocrine differentiation
Tissue Changes: Stromal desmoplasia
Lymphovascular invasion
Perineural invasion
Pleural invasion
Tumor necrosis (especially SCLC)
Lymphocytic infiltration (TILs)
Cellular Characteristics: Adenocarcinoma: columnar cells, glandular architecture, intracytoplasmic mucin
Squamous: polygonal cells, abundant pink cytoplasm, keratin pearls
SCLC: oval cells ~2x lymphocyte size, minimal cytoplasm, crush artifact on biopsy
High mitotic rate in all subtypes

Lung Cancer — Biomarkers

Diagnostic Biomarkers: TTF-1 (Thyroid Transcription Factor-1) — adenocarcinoma marker
p40/p63, CK5/6 — squamous cell carcinoma markers
Synaptophysin, Chromogranin A, CD56 — neuroendocrine/SCLC markers
CEA — adenocarcinoma (serum and IHC)
Napsin A — lung adenocarcinoma-specific
Prognostic Biomarkers: KRAS mutation — historically poor prognosis; KRASG12C now targetable
STK11/LKB1 mutation — reduced immunotherapy benefit
KEAP1 mutation — reduced immunotherapy benefit, poor prognosis
TP53 mutation — adverse prognosis
Stage (TNM) — most important prognostic factor
Performance status (PS)
PD-L1 TPS ≥50% — predictive of pembrolizumab monotherapy benefit
Genetic Markers: EGFR exon 19 del / L858R — sensitive to EGFR TKIs (osimertinib, gefitinib, erlotinib, afatinib)
ALK rearrangement — sensitive to ALK inhibitors (alectinib, crizotinib, brigatinib, lorlatinib)
ROS1 rearrangement — sensitive to crizotinib, entrectinib
BRAF V600E — sensitive to dabrafenib+trametinib
MET exon 14 skipping — sensitive to capmatinib, tepotinib
RET fusion — sensitive to selpercatinib, pralsetinib
NTRK fusion — sensitive to larotrectinib, entrectinib
KRASG12C mutation — sensitive to sotorasib, adagrasib
HER2 mutation — sensitive to trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd)

Lung Cancer — Treatment

First Line Treatment: Stage I-II NSCLC: surgical resection (lobectomy) with curative intent. Stage III NSCLC: concurrent chemoradiation ± durvalumab. Stage IV NSCLC: systemic therapy based on molecular profile and PD-L1 status. SCLC: chemotherapy (etoposide + cisplatin/carboplatin) ± immunotherapy.
Medications: Osimertinib — 3rd generation EGFR TKI (standard of care for EGFR+ NSCLC)
Alectinib — ALK inhibitor (standard of care for ALK+ NSCLC)
Pembrolizumab — anti-PD-1 immunotherapy
Atezolizumab — anti-PD-L1 immunotherapy
Durvalumab — anti-PD-L1 (adjuvant unresectable stage III after chemoRT)
Sotorasib/Adagrasib — KRASG12C inhibitors
Cisplatin/Carboplatin + Pemetrexed — standard doublet chemotherapy for adenocarcinoma
Etoposide + Carboplatin — SCLC first-line
Surgery: Lobectomy — standard curative surgery for stage I-II NSCLC
Sublobar resection (segmentectomy, wedge resection) — stage I in compromised patients
Pneumonectomy — rare, for central tumors
Video-Assisted Thoracoscopic Surgery (VATS) — minimally invasive approach
Robotic-assisted thoracic surgery (RATS)
Mediastinal lymph node dissection — mandatory for accurate staging
Chemotherapy: Carboplatin + Paclitaxel — widely used doublet
Cisplatin + Pemetrexed — non-squamous NSCLC
Cisplatin + Gemcitabine — squamous cell carcinoma
Etoposide + Cisplatin/Carboplatin — SCLC
Docetaxel — second-line NSCLC
Pemetrexed — maintenance therapy in non-squamous NSCLC
Radiotherapy: Stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR/SBRT) — medically inoperable stage I-II
Concurrent chemoradiotherapy — stage III unresectable NSCLC
Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) — limited-stage SCLC with complete response
Palliative radiotherapy — bone pain, SVC syndrome, brain metastases
Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) — limited brain metastases
Immunotherapy: Pembrolizumab monotherapy — PD-L1 TPS ≥50% first-line NSCLC
Pembrolizumab + chemotherapy — PD-L1 any/unselected first-line NSCLC
Atezolizumab + bevacizumab + chemotherapy — first-line non-squamous NSCLC
Nivolumab + ipilimumab ± chemotherapy — first-line NSCLC
Durvalumab — adjuvant after concurrent chemoRT in unresectable stage III
Atezolizumab — adjuvant resected NSCLC (PD-L1+)
Atezolizumab + carboplatin + etoposide — extensive-stage SCLC first-line
Targeted Therapy: EGFR inhibitors: Osimertinib (3rd gen), Erlotinib, Gefitinib, Afatinib
ALK inhibitors: Alectinib, Brigatinib, Lorlatinib, Crizotinib
ROS1 inhibitors: Entrectinib, Crizotinib
BRAF+MEK: Dabrafenib + Trametinib
MET inhibitors: Capmatinib, Tepotinib
RET inhibitors: Selpercatinib, Pralsetinib
NTRK inhibitors: Larotrectinib, Entrectinib
KRASG12C inhibitors: Sotorasib, Adagrasib
HER2-directed: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd)
VEGF inhibitors: Bevacizumab (with chemotherapy)

Lung Cancer — Complications

Short Term: Post-surgical: air leak, pneumonia, atelectasis, bronchopleural fistula
Chemotherapy toxicity: myelosuppression, nausea/vomiting, nephrotoxicity (cisplatin), neuropathy
Radiation pneumonitis
Immune-related adverse events from immunotherapy
Pleural effusion
Hemoptysis
Pulmonary embolism (cancer-associated thrombosis)
Long Term: Post-pneumonectomy syndrome
Radiation fibrosis
Chronic immune-related adverse events (pneumonitis, hypothyroidism)
Acquired resistance to targeted therapy (e.g., EGFR T790M, C797S; ALK resistance mutations)
Brain metastases (very common in adenocarcinoma, SCLC)
Skeletal-related events from bone metastases
Cognitive impairment from brain radiation
Paraneoplastic syndrome complications

Lung Cancer — Prevention

Prevention Methods: Tobacco cessation — single most effective prevention measure
Avoidance of secondhand smoke
Radon testing and mitigation in homes
Occupational safety measures (asbestos abatement, proper respiratory protection)
Air quality improvement policies
Lifestyle Modifications: Smoking cessation (reduces risk; never too late)
Diet rich in fruits and vegetables (potential protective effect)
Regular physical activity
Avoiding indoor air pollutants
Screening Recommendations: Annual low-dose CT (LDCT) screening for high-risk individuals:
— Age 50-80 years, with ≥20 pack-year smoking history, currently smoking or quit within 15 years (USPSTF 2021 recommendations)
— NLST and NELSON trials demonstrated 20-26% reduction in lung cancer mortality
Smoking cessation counseling should accompany screening programs

Lung Cancer — Prognosis

Survival Rate: Stage I: ~60-90% 5-year OS (by subtype); Stage II: ~30-55%; Stage III: ~10-35%; Stage IV: historically <5%, improved to ~20-30% with modern targeted therapy and immunotherapy. SCLC limited-stage: median OS ~15-20 months; extensive-stage: median OS ~10-12 months.
Disease Progression: Lung cancer typically progresses from locoregional disease to distant metastases involving brain, bone, adrenal glands, and liver. EGFR/ALK-mutant patients have prolonged OS on targeted therapy despite metastatic disease. SCLC is characterized by initial chemosensitivity followed by rapid relapse.
Recurrence: Post-surgical recurrence in early-stage NSCLC: ~30-50% within 5 years. Adjuvant osimertinib reduces recurrence in EGFR+ stage IB-IIIA resected NSCLC by ~83%. Post-treatment surveillance with CT scans every 6 months for 2 years then annually.

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) — Basic Information

Disease Name: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM)
Definition: Type 1 diabetes mellitus is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by T-cell mediated destruction of pancreatic beta cells, resulting in absolute insulin deficiency and lifelong dependence on exogenous insulin therapy.
Overview: T1DM accounts for approximately 5-10% of all diabetes cases and typically presents in childhood or young adulthood, though it can occur at any age. The hallmark is the autoimmune obliteration of islet beta cells, leading to severe insulin deficiency. Without insulin, glucose cannot enter cells for energy, and unregulated lipolysis and ketogenesis lead to the acute emergency of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). Management requires insulin replacement, continuous glucose monitoring, and lifestyle adjustment.

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) — Causes

Causes: Autoimmune T-cell mediated destruction of pancreatic beta cells (CD4+ and CD8+ T lymphocytes attack beta cell antigens)
Molecular mimicry triggered by environmental factors (viral infections, dietary antigens)
Genetic susceptibility genes enabling aberrant immune response to self-antigens
Risk Factors: HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR4 alleles (strongest genetic risk factors; present in >90% of T1DM)
Family history of T1DM (sibling risk ~5-10%; offspring of affected father ~6%)
Certain viral infections (Coxsackievirus B, enteroviruses, rubella) — trigger hypothesis
Geography — higher incidence in Northern Europe (Finland highest globally)
Autoimmune diseases in patient or family (Hashimoto's thyroiditis, Addison's disease, celiac disease)
Vitamin D deficiency
Early infant dietary factors (cow's milk protein, early introduction of cereals — controversial)
Gut microbiome dysbiosis

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) — Pathophysiology

Disease Mechanism: T1DM is characterized by a 'pre-diabetic' phase (months to years) of ongoing beta cell autoimmunity before clinical onset. Autoreactive CD8+ T cells recognize beta cell antigens (insulin, GAD65, IA-2) and destroy beta cells. As beta cell mass declines below a critical threshold (~80-90% loss), insulin secretion becomes inadequate to maintain normoglycemia, leading to hyperglycemia. Absolute insulin deficiency causes unrestrained lipolysis, ketone body formation, and if untreated, DKA.
Cellular Changes: Insulitis — mononuclear cell (T cells, macrophages, B cells) infiltration of islets
Progressive beta cell loss; preserved alpha cell (glucagon-secreting) mass
Upregulation of MHC class I on beta cells — increased susceptibility to cytotoxic T-cell killing
Beta cell apoptosis via Fas-FasL pathway and perforin/granzyme release
Pro-inflammatory cytokine release (IL-1β, TNF-α, IFN-γ) causing direct beta cell toxicity
Loss of first-phase insulin secretion — earliest detectable physiological defect

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) — Classification

Types: Type 1A: Classic autoimmune T1DM with detectable autoantibodies (most common); associated with HLA risk alleles.
Type 1B: Idiopathic T1DM — absolute insulin deficiency without evidence of autoimmunity; rare; more common in African and Asian populations.
Lada: Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults — adult-onset T1DM misclassified as T2DM; positive autoantibodies; slower beta cell destruction.
Stages: Stage 1: autoantibody positive, normoglycemia; Stage 2: autoantibody positive, dysglycemia; Stage 3: clinical T1DM (symptomatic hyperglycemia)

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) — Clinical Presentation

Early Symptoms: Polyuria (excessive urination)
Polydipsia (excessive thirst)
Polyphagia (increased hunger)
Unexplained weight loss despite normal or increased appetite
Fatigue
Common Symptoms: Classic triad: polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia
Blurred vision (osmotic lens changes)
Recurrent infections (skin, urinary tract)
Nocturia
Fruity breath (ketones)
Abdominal pain (early DKA)
Advanced Symptoms: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA): nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, Kussmaul breathing, altered consciousness, coma
Severe dehydration
Electrolyte imbalances (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia initially then hypokalemia)
Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (rare in T1DM)

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) — Diagnosis

Physical Examination: Signs of dehydration (DKA presentation)
Kussmaul respiration (deep rapid breathing) in DKA
Fruity/acetone breath odor in DKA
Assessment of BMI (typically normal or low in T1DM)
Laboratory Tests: Blood Tests: Fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL (7.0 mmol/L) on two occasions
Random plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L) with symptoms
HbA1c ≥6.5% (48 mmol/mol) — 3-month glycemic average
C-peptide — very low or undetectable (reflects endogenous insulin secretion)
Autoantibody panel: GADA (anti-GAD65), IA-2A (anti-islet tyrosine phosphatase), IAA (anti-insulin), ZnT8A (anti-zinc transporter 8)
Blood ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate) and urine ketones
Arterial blood gas — metabolic acidosis in DKA
Electrolytes, BUN, creatinine — baseline and DKA management
Thyroid function tests (autoimmune comorbidities screening)
Imaging: Pancreatic imaging not routinely required for diagnosis
Ultrasound/CT to exclude pancreatic pathology if atypical presentation
Biopsy: Pancreatic biopsy not performed in clinical practice; insulitis confirmed only in autopsy or research studies.
Histopathological Examination: Research only: insulitis with mononuclear infiltrate; dramatic reduction in beta cell mass; preserved alpha and delta cells; islet fibrosis in long-standing disease

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) — Histopathology

Microscopic Findings: Insulitis: dense infiltration of islets by T lymphocytes, macrophages, and B cells
Selective beta cell destruction with preservation of other islet cells (alpha, delta, PP cells)
Islet atrophy and fibrosis in long-standing T1DM
Ghost islets (fibrotic remnants devoid of beta cells)
Peri-insulitis early stage: immune cells surrounding but not yet invading islets
Tissue Changes: Progressive reduction in islet volume and beta cell mass
Replacement of islet tissue by fibrous scar tissue
Increased amyloid deposition (less prominent than T2DM)
Exocrine pancreatic atrophy (pancreas slightly reduced in size)
Cellular Characteristics: Beta cells: pyknotic nuclei, apoptotic bodies in active insulitis
Infiltrating cells: CD8+ cytotoxic T cells, CD4+ helper T cells, macrophages, NK cells
MHC class I overexpression on remaining beta cells
Absence of normal dense, granular beta cells by Grimelius stain or insulin IHC

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) — Biomarkers

Diagnostic Biomarkers: GADA (Glutamic Acid Decarboxylase Antibodies) — most sensitive autoantibody (~80%)
IA-2A (Islet Antigen 2 Antibodies) — ~60% sensitivity
IAA (Insulin Autoantibodies) — most common in young children at onset
ZnT8A (Zinc Transporter 8 Antibodies) — ~60-80% sensitivity
ICA (Islet Cell Antibodies) — historically used, now supplemented by specific antibodies
C-peptide — low/undetectable confirming beta cell failure
Prognostic Biomarkers: HbA1c — glycemic control and complication risk
Residual C-peptide — even small amounts associated with better outcomes and reduced DKA risk
eGFR and urine albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) — renal complication monitoring
Lipid panel — cardiovascular risk
Time in range (TIR) >70% by continuous glucose monitor (CGM) — modern glycemic target
Genetic Markers: HLA-DR3-DQ2 and HLA-DR4-DQ8 — highest genetic risk haplotypes
HLA-DR15-DQ6 (DQB1*0602) — protective haplotype
INS VNTR — insulin gene region; modifies risk
PTPN22 — protein tyrosine phosphatase; autoimmunity risk gene
CTLA4 — immune regulatory gene
IL2RA (CD25) — T-cell regulation
Polygenic risk scores (PRS) for T1DM prediction

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) — Treatment

First Line Treatment: Insulin replacement therapy is mandatory and lifelong. Multiple daily injections (MDI) or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII/insulin pump) are the two main delivery modes.
Medications: Rapid-acting insulin analogs: Lispro (Humalog), Aspart (NovoLog), Glulisine (Apidra), Ultra-rapid Fiasp
Long-acting basal insulins: Glargine (Lantus, Toujeo), Detemir (Levemir), Degludec (Tresiba)
Premixed insulins: limited use in T1DM
Teplizumab (Tzield) — FDA-approved (2022) anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody to delay clinical T1DM in Stage 2 high-risk individuals
Insulin Therapy: Multiple Daily Injections: Basal-bolus regimen: long-acting insulin once/twice daily + rapid-acting insulin with each meal; carbohydrate counting with insulin:carb ratio
Insulin Pump Csii: Continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion via pump; delivers basal and bolus insulin; improved flexibility and glycemic control
Automated Insulin Delivery: Closed-loop 'artificial pancreas' systems (e.g., Control-IQ, Omnipod 5); CGM-integrated insulin pump with automated adjustments
Targets: HbA1c <7% (<53 mmol/mol) for most adults; individualized targets; Time in Range (TIR) >70% (70-180 mg/dL)
Lifestyle Management: Carbohydrate counting and insulin:carb ratio calculation
Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) use — Dexcom, Libre, Enlite
Regular physical activity with glucose monitoring adjustments
Hypoglycemia recognition and treatment (15g fast-acting carbs; glucagon kit)
Sick day management rules
Annual screening for complications

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) — Complications

Short Term: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) — pH <7.3, ketones positive, anion gap metabolic acidosis; life-threatening emergency
Hypoglycemia — blood glucose <70 mg/dL; severe hypoglycemia may cause loss of consciousness, seizures
Hypoglycemia unawareness — loss of autonomic warning symptoms
Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (rare in T1DM)
Electrolyte disturbances during DKA treatment (hypokalemia)
Long Term: Diabetic nephropathy — leading cause of chronic kidney disease; progression to ESRD
Diabetic retinopathy — leading cause of blindness in working-age adults; proliferative retinopathy, macular edema
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy — painful/painless peripheral neuropathy; diabetic foot ulcers; amputation risk
Diabetic autonomic neuropathy — gastroparesis, orthostatic hypotension, cardiac autonomic neuropathy
Cardiovascular disease — 2-5x increased risk of MI, stroke, heart failure
Celiac disease — increased co-occurrence (5% of T1DM)
Thyroid disease (autoimmune thyroiditis) — 15-30% of T1DM
Addison's disease — rare but increased autoimmune co-occurrence
Lipodystrophy at injection sites

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) — Prevention

Prevention Methods: Primary prevention: no established prevention for T1DM autoimmunity onset in general population
Teplizumab therapy can delay clinical T1DM onset by ~2-3 years in Stage 2 T1DM (seropositive + dysglycemia)
TrialNet screening for autoantibodies in first-degree relatives
Prevention of complications through excellent glycemic control from diagnosis
Lifestyle Modifications: Consistent meal timing and carbohydrate counting
Regular exercise (aerobic and resistance training with glucose monitoring)
Blood pressure control (<130/80 mmHg)
Smoking cessation
Regular foot examination and foot care
Annual ophthalmologic examination
Screening Recommendations: Annual urine albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) from 5 years after diagnosis
Annual serum creatinine and eGFR
Annual dilated ophthalmologic examination (from 5 years after diagnosis or age 10, whichever earlier)
Annual foot examination and sensory testing (10-g monofilament, vibration)
Lipid panel annually
Blood pressure at every visit
Annual thyroid function testing
Celiac disease antibody screening at diagnosis and when symptomatic

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) — Prognosis

Survival Rate: Modern T1DM management has dramatically improved prognosis. Life expectancy gap between T1DM and general population has narrowed significantly but remains ~10-15 years shorter in some studies. Excellent glycemic control (HbA1c near normal) prevents or delays complications (DCCT/EDIC trial).
Disease Progression: T1DM is lifelong. Without treatment, DKA occurs within days to weeks. Microvascular complications begin accumulating after 5-10 years of poor glycemic control. Intensive glycemic management can halt or reverse early nephropathy and retinopathy.
Recurrence: Not applicable as a primary disease occurrence, but islet transplantation recipients can experience graft failure and return to insulin dependence.

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) — Basic Information

Disease Name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM)
Definition: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder characterized by progressive insulin resistance in peripheral tissues coupled with relative insulin deficiency due to beta cell dysfunction, leading to chronic hyperglycemia.
Overview: T2DM is the most common form of diabetes, accounting for ~90-95% of all cases worldwide. It is strongly associated with obesity, physical inactivity, and aging. Unlike T1DM, T2DM involves insulin resistance rather than absolute insulin deficiency, and initially can be managed with lifestyle intervention and oral medications before insulin therapy becomes necessary. It is a major driver of cardiovascular disease, kidney failure, blindness, and limb amputation globally.

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) — Causes

Causes: Insulin resistance: impaired insulin signaling in skeletal muscle, liver, and adipose tissue
Progressive beta cell dysfunction and eventual beta cell failure
Hepatic glucose overproduction (gluconeogenesis) due to liver insulin resistance
Abnormal incretin effect (reduced GLP-1 secretion/response)
Increased glucagon secretion (alpha cell dysfunction)
Renal glucose reabsorption (increased renal glucose threshold)
Central nervous system dysregulation of appetite and glucose homeostasis
Risk Factors: Overweight or obesity (BMI >25 kg/m², or lower thresholds in Asian populations)
Central/abdominal adiposity (waist circumference >102cm men, >88cm women)
Physical inactivity
Age >45 years (risk increases with age)
Family history of T2DM (first-degree relative)
History of gestational diabetes or delivery of baby >9 lbs
Prediabetes (impaired fasting glucose or impaired glucose tolerance)
Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)
Hypertension
Dyslipidemia (low HDL, high triglycerides)
Metabolic syndrome
Certain racial/ethnic groups (African American, Latino, Native American, Asian American, Pacific Islander)
Sleep apnea
Certain medications (corticosteroids, atypical antipsychotics, thiazide diuretics)

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) — Pathophysiology

Disease Mechanism: T2DM results from a complex interplay of insulin resistance and beta cell dysfunction ('ominous octet' by Ralph DeFronzo). Insulin resistance in skeletal muscle reduces glucose uptake after meals. The liver fails to suppress glucose production (gluconeogenesis) despite high insulin levels. Initially, the pancreas compensates by hypersecretion of insulin, but over time beta cells fail due to glucotoxicity, lipotoxicity, endoplasmic reticulum stress, and amyloid deposition. The resulting relative insulin deficiency combined with insulin resistance causes chronic hyperglycemia.
Cellular Changes: Defective IRS-1/IRS-2 insulin receptor signaling (serine phosphorylation inhibits signaling)
Impaired PI3K-AKT pathway activity in muscle and liver
Increased hepatic glucose production (PEPCK, G6Pase upregulation)
Ectopic lipid deposition in muscle and liver causing lipotoxicity
Beta cell amyloid deposition (IAPP/amylin aggregation)
Beta cell apoptosis from glucotoxicity, lipotoxicity, ER stress
Reduced beta cell mass (~50% at clinical diagnosis)
Impaired GLP-1 and GIP incretin responses
Increased alpha cell glucagon secretion — worsens postprandial hyperglycemia
Adipose tissue inflammation (M1 macrophage infiltration, pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion: TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, resistin)

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) — Classification

Types: Prediabetes: Impaired Fasting Glucose (IFG): FPG 100-125 mg/dL; Impaired Glucose Tolerance (IGT): 2h OGTT 140-199 mg/dL; HbA1c 5.7-6.4%
T2Dm: Full type 2 diabetes with hyperglycemia meeting diagnostic thresholds
Mody: Maturity-Onset Diabetes of the Young — monogenic diabetes, often misclassified as T2DM; caused by single gene mutations (GCK, HNF1A, HNF4A, etc.)
Secondary Diabetes: Drug-induced (corticosteroids), pancreatic disease (pancreatitis, pancreatectomy), endocrine disorders (Cushing's, acromegaly, pheochromocytoma)

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) — Clinical Presentation

Early Symptoms: Often asymptomatic for years — detected on routine screening
Mild fatigue
Increased thirst and urination (mild)
Increased hunger
Common Symptoms: Polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia (classic triad; less dramatic than T1DM)
Weight gain or difficulty losing weight
Fatigue and lack of energy
Blurred vision
Slow-healing wounds or sores
Recurrent infections (skin, urinary, vaginal yeast infections)
Numbness/tingling in hands and feet (neuropathy)
Acanthosis nigricans (darkened skin folds in neck, axilla — sign of insulin resistance)
Advanced Symptoms: Diabetic foot ulcers or non-healing wounds
Symptoms of cardiovascular disease (angina, dyspnea)
Visual loss (retinopathy)
Edema (nephrotic syndrome from nephropathy)
Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (HHS) — extremely high glucose without ketosis; confusion, coma
Peripheral arterial disease symptoms (claudication, cold extremities)

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) — Diagnosis

Physical Examination: BMI calculation and abdominal circumference
Blood pressure measurement
Skin examination (acanthosis nigricans, skin tags, infections)
Foot examination (neuropathy, pulses, skin integrity)
Fundoscopic examination (retinopathy)
Laboratory Tests: Blood Tests: Fasting Plasma Glucose (FPG) ≥126 mg/dL (7.0 mmol/L) on two occasions
2-hour OGTT (75g glucose) ≥200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L)
HbA1c ≥6.5% (48 mmol/mol)
Random glucose ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms
C-peptide — normal or elevated (distinguishes from T1DM)
Lipid panel — triglycerides, HDL, LDL, total cholesterol
Urine albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) — nephropathy screening
Serum creatinine and eGFR — renal function
Liver function tests (fatty liver is common comorbidity)
TSH — thyroid disease screening
Imaging: Carotid intima media thickness (IMT) — cardiovascular risk assessment
Coronary calcium score — cardiovascular risk stratification
Renal ultrasound — if significant nephropathy
Lower extremity Doppler — peripheral arterial disease
Biopsy: Not routinely performed for T2DM diagnosis. Liver biopsy may be considered for NASH/NAFLD assessment.
Histopathological Examination: Research: islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP) deposition; reduced beta cell mass; beta cell hypertrophy (compensatory phase); islet fibrosis

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) — Histopathology

Microscopic Findings: Islet amyloid deposition (IAPP/amylin) — characteristic finding, present in >90% of T2DM
Reduced beta cell mass (~40-60% of normal)
Islet fibrosis
Beta cell hypertrophy (compensatory phase)
Relatively preserved alpha cells
Vacuolar degeneration of beta cells
Diffuse islet atrophy in long-standing T2DM
Tissue Changes: Increased adipose tissue infiltration around islets
Peri-islet fibrosis
Atherosclerosis in pancreatic vessels
Exocrine pancreas relatively unaffected (unlike T1DM)
Liver: hepatic steatosis (NAFLD/NASH) very common comorbidity
Cellular Characteristics: Beta cells with pale cytoplasm and enlarged ER (ER stress)
Amyloid plaques replacing islet architecture
Mitochondrial dysfunction in beta cells
Absence of significant mononuclear infiltration (unlike T1DM)

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) — Biomarkers

Diagnostic Biomarkers: HbA1c ≥6.5% — gold standard for diagnosis and monitoring
Fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL
2-hour 75g OGTT glucose ≥200 mg/dL
Fasting insulin and C-peptide — elevated early (insulin resistance), declining later (beta cell failure)

Urine albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) — early nephropathy

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) — Treatment

First Line Treatment: Lifestyle intervention (diet modification, weight loss, physical activity) plus Metformin as initial pharmacotherapy (unless contraindicated).
Medications: Metformin — first-line; reduces hepatic glucose production; weight-neutral/loss; cardioprotective
GLP-1 receptor agonists: Semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy), Liraglutide (Victoza), Dulaglutide, Tirzepatide (GIP/GLP-1 dual agonist) — weight loss, cardiovascular benefit
SGLT2 inhibitors: Empagliflozin (Jardiance), Dapagliflozin (Farxiga), Canagliflozin — cardiovascular and renal protection, weight loss
DPP-4 inhibitors: Sitagliptin (Januvia), Saxagliptin, Linagliptin — weight-neutral
Sulfonylureas: Glipizide, Glimepiride, Glyburide — insulin secretagogues; hypoglycemia risk; weight gain
Thiazolidinediones (TZDs): Pioglitazone — improves insulin sensitivity; weight gain; fluid retention
Insulin: NPH, glargine, detemir, degludec; rapid-acting analogs as needed
Tirzepatide (Mounjaro) — dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist; superior HbA1c and weight reduction
Oral Medications: Metformin 500-2000 mg/day (first-line)
SGLT2 inhibitors — especially if HF, CKD, or CVD present
GLP-1 RAs — especially if obesity or CVD present
DPP-4 inhibitors — weight-neutral, well-tolerated
Sulfonylureas — cost-effective but hypoglycemia risk
Acarbose (alpha-glucosidase inhibitor) — postprandial glucose control
Insulin Therapy: Basal insulin added when oral agents insufficient (target HbA1c not achieved). Progression to basal-bolus regimen if needed. Insulin degludec (Tresiba) or glargine U-300 (Toujeo) have lower hypoglycemia risk.
Lifestyle Management: Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT): caloric restriction for weight loss; carbohydrate quality and quantity; Mediterranean or DASH diet
Physical activity: 150 min/week moderate aerobic + 2x/week resistance training
Weight loss: 5-10% body weight reduction significantly improves glycemia; >15% may achieve remission
Bariatric/metabolic surgery: for BMI ≥40 or ≥35 with comorbidities; can achieve T2DM remission
Smoking cessation
Blood pressure target <130/80 mmHg
Lipid management: statin therapy for most T2DM adults

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) — Complications

Short Term: Hypoglycemia (from sulfonylureas or insulin)
Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State (HHS) — extremely high glucose (>600 mg/dL), severe dehydration, altered consciousness
Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) — rare in T2DM but possible especially with SGLT2 inhibitors (euglycemic DKA)
Medication side effects: lactic acidosis with metformin (rare), UTI/genital infections with SGLT2i
Long Term: Microvascular: Diabetic nephropathy (leading cause of ESRD), Diabetic retinopathy (leading cause of blindness), Diabetic peripheral and autonomic neuropathy
Macrovascular: Coronary artery disease/MI, Stroke, Peripheral arterial disease
Diabetic foot disease: neuropathic/ischemic ulcers, Charcot arthropathy, amputation
Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) / NASH / cirrhosis
Erectile dysfunction and sexual dysfunction
Increased cancer risk (liver, pancreas, colorectal, breast, endometrial)
Cognitive decline and dementia
Obstructive sleep apnea
Depression and anxiety

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) — Prevention

Prevention Methods: Lifestyle intervention in prediabetes reduces T2DM risk by 58% (Diabetes Prevention Program)
Metformin therapy in high-risk prediabetes reduces progression
Weight loss of 5-10% body weight
Regular physical activity
Healthy dietary patterns
Lifestyle Modifications: Mediterranean, DASH, or low-glycemic index diet
150+ minutes of moderate physical activity per week
Achieve and maintain healthy body weight
Reduce sedentary behavior
Moderate alcohol consumption
Smoking cessation
Adequate sleep (7-9 hours)
Screening Recommendations: Screen all adults ≥35 years with overweight/obesity
Screen at any age if: BMI ≥25 + one or more risk factors (ADA 2024)
Testing: FPG, 2h OGTT, or HbA1c
If normal: rescreen every 3 years; if prediabetes: rescreen annually
Screen children/adolescents with overweight + risk factors
Screen gestational diabetes patients within 4-12 weeks postpartum and every 1-3 years thereafter

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) — Prognosis

Survival Rate: T2DM significantly reduces life expectancy (average ~10 years reduction without optimal management). Optimal management of glycemia, blood pressure, lipids, and lifestyle can near-normalize cardiovascular risk. Modern therapies (GLP-1 RAs, SGLT2i) have demonstrated cardiovascular and renal mortality benefits.
Disease Progression: T2DM is progressive: beta cell function declines ~4-6% per year. Most patients require addition of medications over time. After 10 years, many patients need insulin. Complications develop over years of hyperglycemia exposure; microvascular complications can appear even at prediabetes stage.
Recurrence: T2DM remission is possible with bariatric surgery (50-80% remission at 1 year) or very low-calorie diet (DiRECT trial: 46% remission at 1 year). Remission defined as HbA1c <6.5% off glucose-lowering medications for ≥3 months.

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) — Basic Information

Disease Name: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM)
Definition: Gestational diabetes mellitus is defined as glucose intolerance with onset or first recognition during pregnancy, typically in the second or third trimester, in women not previously known to have diabetes.
Overview: GDM is the most common metabolic complication of pregnancy, affecting 6-16% of pregnancies worldwide (WHO estimate). It arises from pregnancy-induced insulin resistance exacerbating pre-existing subclinical metabolic defects in susceptible women. GDM carries significant risks for both mother (pre-eclampsia, cesarean delivery, future T2DM) and fetus/neonate (macrosomia, birth injuries, neonatal hypoglycemia, future obesity and diabetes). Most cases resolve after delivery, but affected women have up to 50% lifetime risk of developing T2DM.

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) — Causes

Causes: Pregnancy-induced insulin resistance (physiological — from placental hormones: human placental lactogen, progesterone, cortisol, estrogen, prolactin)
Failure of maternal pancreatic beta cells to adequately compensate for increased insulin demand
Underlying pre-existing insulin resistance unmasked by pregnancy
Genetic predisposition to beta cell dysfunction
Risk Factors: Overweight or obesity (BMI >25 kg/m²) before pregnancy
Maternal age >25-35 years
Family history of T2DM (first-degree relative)
Prior history of GDM
Prior macrosomic baby (birth weight >4 kg)
Prior stillbirth or fetal loss
Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)
Certain ethnic/racial groups (South Asian, East Asian, African American, Hispanic, Native American, Pacific Islander)
Multiple gestation
Glucocorticoid use during pregnancy
Excessive gestational weight gain
Prediabetes before pregnancy
Sedentary lifestyle and unhealthy diet

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) — Pathophysiology

Disease Mechanism: Normal pregnancy induces progressive insulin resistance beginning in the second trimester, peaking in the third trimester, to ensure adequate glucose supply to the growing fetus. Healthy women compensate through 200-250% increase in insulin secretion. In GDM, beta cells fail to provide adequate insulin secretion to overcome placental hormone-induced insulin resistance (human placental lactogen being the dominant driver). This results in postprandial hyperglycemia that crosses the placenta via glucose transporters, causing fetal hyperinsulinemia and macrosomia.
Cellular Changes: Placental hormones activate serine kinases that phosphorylate IRS-1, impairing insulin signaling
Reduced GLUT4 translocation to skeletal muscle cell membranes (decreased glucose uptake)
Increased hepatic glucose production despite elevated insulin levels
Impaired first-phase insulin secretion (earliest detectable beta cell defect)
Fetal hyperinsulinemia stimulates anabolic growth (macrosomia, organomegaly)
Adipokine dysregulation: decreased adiponectin, increased resistin, TNF-α, leptin
Placental inflammation and oxidative stress contributing to insulin resistance

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) — Classification

Types: Gdm Diet A1: Class A1 GDM — controlled with diet and lifestyle modification alone; fasting glucose <95 mg/dL and 1h <140 mg/dL and 2h <120 mg/dL after meal; ADA classification.
Gdm Insulin A2: Class A2 GDM — requires insulin or oral hypoglycemic agents to achieve glycemic targets; greater fetal/maternal risk.
Overt Diabetes: Pre-existing undiagnosed diabetes discovered in first trimester — distinguished from GDM by earlier onset and higher hyperglycemia; managed as pregestational diabetes.

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) — Clinical Presentation

Early Symptoms: Usually asymptomatic — detected on routine prenatal screening (24-28 weeks)
Mild polydipsia or polyuria (may be attributed to normal pregnancy)
Increased fatigue
Common Symptoms: Typically asymptomatic — diagnosed through screening
Polyuria, polydipsia (if significantly elevated glucose)
Recurrent vaginal yeast infections
Fatigue and malaise
Blurred vision
Advanced Symptoms: Symptoms related to macrosomic fetus: large fundal height, fetal movement changes
Signs of pre-eclampsia: hypertension, proteinuria, edema
Rare: DKA (very rare in GDM, more likely in unrecognized T1DM)
Fetal complications: decreased fetal movements (fetal distress), polyhydramnios

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) — Diagnosis

Physical Examination: Symphysis-fundal height measurement — large for gestational age (macrosomia)
Blood pressure assessment — hypertension/pre-eclampsia
Weight gain monitoring — excessive gestational weight gain
Edema assessment
Laboratory Tests: Blood Tests: One-Step Strategy (IADPSG/WHO): 75g OGTT at 24-28 weeks — GDM diagnosed if: Fasting ≥92 mg/dL (5.1 mmol/L) OR 1h ≥180 mg/dL (10.0 mmol/L) OR 2h ≥153 mg/dL (8.5 mmol/L)
Two-Step Strategy (ADA/ACOG): Step 1: 50g GCT (non-fasting) — if 1h ≥130/135/140 mg/dL → proceed to Step 2; Step 2: 100g OGTT fasting — GDM if 2+ values meet thresholds (Carpenter-Coustan or NDDG criteria)
HbA1c at first prenatal visit (if not recently tested) — screen for pre-existing diabetes
Fasting plasma glucose at first visit — overt diabetes if ≥126 mg/dL
Urine dipstick for glucose and protein — monitoring during visits
Complete blood count, thyroid function
Urine albumin — renal function
Imaging: Obstetric ultrasound — fetal biometry (estimated fetal weight, abdominal circumference) to detect macrosomia
Doppler velocimetry — placental function assessment if growth concerns
Non-stress test (NST) and biophysical profile (BPP) — fetal well-being in poorly controlled GDM
Biopsy: Not applicable in clinical GDM management.
Histopathological Examination: Placental pathology post-delivery (research): increased syncytiotrophoblast oxidative stress; inflammatory changes; impaired angiogenesis; chorangiosis (increased villous vascularity from chronic hypoxia in uncontrolled GDM)

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) — Histopathology

Microscopic Findings: Placenta: chorangiosis (>10 capillaries per villus in >10 villi) — from chronic hyperglycemia-induced hypoxia
Placental hydrops and edema of villi
Decidual vasculopathy in severe cases with hypertension
Increased syncytiotrophoblast apoptosis
Villous immaturity (relative to gestational age)
Tissue Changes: Placental enlargement (hyperplacentosis)
Umbilical cord edema
Amniotic fluid: polyhydramnios reflected in membrane changes
Neonatal pancreas: beta cell hyperplasia and hypertrophy (fetal hyperinsulinemia response)
Cellular Characteristics: Fetal beta cell hyperplasia — compensatory response to maternal hyperglycemia/fetal hyperglycemia
Maternal islet hypertrophy — compensation for insulin resistance
Placental trophoblast: altered GLUT1 and GLUT3 expression
Inflammatory macrophage infiltration in placental villi

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) — Biomarkers

Diagnostic Biomarkers: Fasting plasma glucose — key screening and diagnostic value
75g OGTT (1h and 2h glucose values) — IADPSG diagnostic criteria
HbA1c — used first trimester for screening overt diabetes; less reliable during pregnancy due to RBC turnover changes
Fructosamine — reflects 2-3 week glycemic average; useful when HbA1c unreliable
C-peptide — reflects residual beta cell function
Prognostic Biomarkers: Fasting hyperglycemia >95 mg/dL — higher risk of adverse outcomes; likely needs medication
Degree of hyperglycemia at diagnosis — predictive of insulin requirement
Gestational weight gain — excess predicts macrosomia
HbA1c postpartum — predicts future T2DM risk
Adiponectin levels — low levels predict GDM and future T2DM
Ferritin — high levels associated with GDM risk
Vitamin D levels — deficiency associated with GDM
Genetic Markers: TCF7L2 — strongest T2DM risk gene; also increases GDM risk
MTNR1B — melatonin receptor; regulates insulin secretion; GDM risk gene
GCK (glucokinase) — mutations cause MODY2, presenting as GDM
IRS1 — insulin receptor substrate gene variants
PPARG, KCNJ11 — overlap with T2DM risk genes
HLA risk alleles — some GDM cases represent latent T1DM (LADA) onset in pregnancy

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) — Treatment

First Line Treatment: Blood glucose self-monitoring + Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT) + moderate physical activity. Insulin therapy added if glycemic targets not met within 1-2 weeks on lifestyle alone.
Medications: Insulin — gold standard pharmacotherapy in GDM; does not cross placenta; NPH (intermediate-acting) and regular insulin; rapid analogs (Lispro, Aspart) safe in pregnancy
Metformin — used in some countries (UK, Australia) as oral alternative; crosses placenta; long-term neonatal safety data reassuring but still controversial per ADA
Glyburide (Glibenclamide) — oral sulfonylurea; does cross placenta; less preferred; associated with higher neonatal hypoglycemia risk
Insulin Therapy: Starting doses based on weight and trimester. Typically: NPH at bedtime for fasting hyperglycemia; rapid-acting analog before meals for postprandial control. Dose adjustment weekly based on glucose logs. Target: Fasting <95 mg/dL (5.3 mmol/L); 1h postmeal <140 mg/dL (7.8 mmol/L); 2h postmeal <120 mg/dL (6.7 mmol/L) — per ADA.
Lifestyle Management: Medical Nutrition Therapy: carbohydrate distribution throughout day; avoid refined sugars; adequate protein and healthy fat; 1800-2500 kcal/day adjusted to BMI; carbohydrate 35-45% of calories
Physical activity: 30 min moderate intensity activity (walking) daily or most days
Blood glucose self-monitoring: 4-6 times daily (fasting + after meals)
Weight gain targets: <5 kg additional weight gain if obese
Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) in insulin-requiring GDM
Avoid ketogenic diets during pregnancy

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) — Complications

Short Term: Maternal: Pre-eclampsia (2-4x increased risk), polyhydramnios, cesarean delivery, urinary tract infections, preterm labor
Fetal/Neonatal: Macrosomia (birthweight >4kg), birth trauma (shoulder dystocia, brachial plexus injury, fractures), neonatal hypoglycemia (most common neonatal complication), neonatal respiratory distress syndrome, neonatal polycythemia, neonatal jaundice (hyperbilirubinemia), intrauterine fetal demise (rare with uncontrolled GDM)
Iatrogenic preterm birth (induction of labor)
Maternal hypoglycemia (from insulin therapy)
Long Term: Maternal: 50% lifetime risk of T2DM; recurrence of GDM in subsequent pregnancies (30-70%); increased metabolic syndrome risk; cardiovascular disease risk
Offspring: 5-10x increased risk of T2DM and obesity in childhood/adulthood; increased risk of metabolic syndrome; impaired cognitive development if severe uncontrolled GDM; possible increased cardiovascular risk in adulthood (Barker hypothesis/developmental programming)

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) — Prevention

Prevention Methods: Pre-conception weight optimization (achieve healthy BMI before pregnancy)
Regular physical activity before and during pregnancy
Healthy diet pre-conception and during pregnancy
Management of PCOS and prediabetes before conception
Myo-inositol supplementation (evidence-emerging; may reduce GDM risk in high-risk women)
Vitamin D supplementation (observational evidence)
Lifestyle Modifications: Mediterranean-style diet with complex carbohydrates, fiber, healthy fats
Regular moderate physical activity throughout pregnancy
Appropriate gestational weight gain per IOM guidelines
Breastfeeding (reduces postpartum T2DM risk for mother and reduces childhood obesity in offspring)
Screening Recommendations: Universal screening at 24-28 weeks gestation — WHO, IADPSG, most guidelines
Early screening at first prenatal visit for high-risk women (BMI >30, prior GDM, strong family history, previous macrosomic baby)
Postpartum screening: 75g OGTT at 4-12 weeks postpartum (ADA 2024)
Annual follow-up with HbA1c or FPG for all GDM-affected women
Lifestyle intervention in GDM-affected women to prevent progression to T2DM
Offspring monitoring: screen children for obesity and metabolic risk from early childhood

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) — Prognosis

Survival Rate: GDM itself is not life-threatening when well-managed. With appropriate treatment, outcomes approach those of non-diabetic pregnancies. Poorly controlled GDM significantly increases risk of stillbirth and neonatal complications.
Disease Progression: GDM typically resolves after delivery as placental hormones clear. However, it is a sentinel event for future metabolic disease: ~50% of women with GDM will develop T2DM within 5-10 years. Risk is highest in the first 5 years postpartum. Breastfeeding, weight loss, and physical activity reduce conversion risk.
Recurrence: GDM recurs in 30-70% of subsequent pregnancies. Risk factors for recurrence include: insulin use in prior GDM pregnancy, higher BMI, interpregnancy weight gain, Hispanic ethnicity, and shorter interpregnancy interval.
